input_candidates.truth_value: The initial dataset from MatWeb contained 173 data points; after outlier removal and CGAN augmentation, expanded to 192 points.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Dataset construction: 173 Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel data points were initially collected from MatWeb; after Z-score and isolation forest outlier removal, 98 data points remained; CGANs with double hidden layers expanded the dataset to 192 data points.

input_candidates.truth_value: Among 10 ML models, SVR achieved R²=0.85, RMSE=0.39; after incorporating modified solid solution strengthening, R² improved to 0.89, RMSE reduced to 0.31.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Feature engineering: 41 features were collected and standardized; recursive feature elimination (RFE) wrapped with RF, LightGBM and XGBoost, combined with Pearson correlation screening (|PCC| ≥ 0.95), reduced candidates to 9; exhaustive screening identified the optimal 3-feature combination {δ_Ec, D_Ec, G} with the lowest cross-validation RMSE of 0.257.

input_candidates.truth_value: Feature engineering revealed that shear modulus G, difference in cohesive energy δ_Ec, and local cohesive energy mismatch D_Ec are the key features for microhardness.
input_candidates.prediction_value: ML model comparison before solid-solution modification: SVR (R² = 0.85, RMSE = 0.39), ET (0.80, 0.45), MLP (0.81, 0.44), Ridge (0.82, 0.43), LightGBM (0.63, 0.62), XGBoost (0.79, 0.46), RF (0.74, 0.52), LR (0.76, 0.48), DT (0.72, 0.54).

input_candidates.truth_value: Cr, Ni, Mo were identified as elements with positive impact on microhardness, while Mn, V, W showed weaker correlation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Inverse design optimization predicted maximum microhardness: FA 733.59 HV, PSO 730.04 HV, GWO 708.60 HV, SA 700.37 HV; FA was selected for the highest predicted hardness.

input_candidates.truth_value: Inverse design using FA achieved a predicted maximum microhardness of 733.59 HV.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Experimental validation errors: Sample 1 predicted 521 HV versus experimental 515 HV (1.17 % error); Sample 2 predicted 490 HV versus 500 HV (2.00 %); Sample 3 predicted 415 HV versus 424 HV (2.09 %); the minimum error of 1.17 % is lower than previously reported 2.01 % (Ren et al.) and 2.96 % (Huang et al.) for high-entropy alloy hardness prediction.

input_candidates.truth_value: The δ_r and ΔH_mix values for the three samples fall within the ranges reported for solid solution phase formation (δ_r < 0.066 and -11.6 < ΔH_mix < 3.2 kJ/mol).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Elemental impact on microhardness from data correlation: Cr exhibited the strongest positive correlation with microhardness, followed by Ni and Mo; Mn, V and W showed weaker correlations.

input_candidates.truth_value: CGAN generator and discriminator architectures detailed in Table 1.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Solid solution formation criteria for the experimental alloys: δ_r ranged 0.0335–0.0595 (below the 0.066 threshold) and ΔH_mix ranged -1.213 to -0.451 kJ/mol (within the -11.6 to 3.2 kJ/mol range), consistent with complete solid solution formation.

input_candidates.truth_value: Hardness test conditions: load 1 kg, dwell time 20 s.
input_candidates.prediction_value: XRD detail note: Fig. 8 caption/table labels Sample 1 peaks as (1 1 0) at ~45°, (2 0 0) at ~65°, (2 1 1) at ~82°, whereas the body text describes Sample 1 as Fe-Cr with diffraction peaks 100, 200, 211 in an FCC lattice; Samples 2 and 3 are described as Fe-Cr-Ni phase.
